trajectory_summary: This is a short recycled-lead call that lands a conditional callback booking. Randy captured meaningful qualification data — existing solar, owned system, loan financing, battery interest confirmed — but the call's structural weakness is that Elwood's stated unresolved questions were never surfaced, leaving the closer to walk in blind on what those questions actually are. The booking is real but the handoff is thin.
outcome_dispute: The v1 tag is 'unknown' — the call ends with a same-day callback booked with a named specialist (Elijah) in a defined window (4–5 PM), with a pre-call text promised. This is a tentative appointment, not a confirmed sit. The booking is conditional on Elwood being reachable in a noisy plant environment, and no verbal handshake ('is that fair?') was obtained. Tag should read: tentative_callback_booked, fragile.

primary_coaching_focus.topic: Handoff context capture — asking what the questions are before routing
primary_coaching_focus.quote: I don't remember who it was. I thought I talked to somebody. But I have some additional questions
primary_coaching_focus.why: Randy correctly identified that the specialist should handle Elwood's questions, but he routed without capturing what those questions are. The closer walks in knowing Elwood wants a battery and has prior Ion contact — but not what he actually wants to discuss. One setter-appropriate question ('What are the main things you're hoping to get answered?') closes this gap without scope creep. This is the highest-leverage coaching beat on this call because it recurs as a pattern: Randy moved to scheduling at every opportunity without pausing to capture context that would make the handoff actionable.

key_moments.quote: We just need to see your Who are you? Who what company are you?
key_moments.stewart_read: Elwood interrupts mid-flow to run a legitimacy check. This is the scam-anxiety signal the schema flags — a customer who will not proceed until they know who they're talking to. Randy's response at 01:12 ('Yeah. This is, Randy with Ion Solar.') is clean, direct, and immediate. The check resolves in one exchange. This is a positive coaching moment: Randy did not get defensive, did not over-explain, and did not stall.

key_moments.quote: Top tier. And did you did you get the panels through a loan or lease?
key_moments.stewart_read: Randy correctly executes the orphaned flow qualification sequence — he identifies the existing solar provider, confirms ownership structure (loan, not lease/PPA), and establishes that Elwood owns the system. This is procedurally correct: a leased or PPA system would be a hard DQ path, and Randy checks it cleanly. The execution is efficient and conversational.

key_moments.quote: Yeah. Someone called me about a battery.
key_moments.stewart_read: Elwood opens the call by naming battery storage as his interest — this is an inbound, purpose-driven signal from a customer who already has solar and is actively seeking to expand his system. The Tesla Powerwall is the only battery brand Ion carries (confirmed in the schema). Randy acknowledges the battery interest and routes to a specialist, which is the correct setter-scope move. However, Randy never asks the one setter-appropriate question that would have made the handoff materially stronger: what is driving the battery interest?

key_moments.quote: I don't remember who it was. I thought I talked to somebody. But I have some additional questions
key_moments.stewart_read: Elwood explicitly names unresolved questions from a prior Ion interaction. Randy's response is to pivot immediately to scheduling ('I can I can set up a time where you can kinda go over that information with him?'). The pivot is not wrong — routing to the specialist is correct setter scope — but Randy does not ask what the questions are, which means the closer has no advance context on what Elwood actually wants to discuss.
